sanskrit_devanagari:
कार्पण्यदोषोपहतस्वभावः
पृच्छामि त्वां धर्मसम्मूढचेताः ।
यच्छ्रेयः स्यान्निश्चितं ब्रूहि तन्मे
शिष्यस्तेऽहं शाधि मां त्वां प्रपन्नम् ॥ ७ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
kārpaṇya-doṣopahata-svabhāvaḥ
pṛcchāmi tvāṁ dharma-saṁmūḍha-cetāḥ |
yac chreyaḥ syān niścitaṁ brūhi tan me
śiṣyas te 'haṁ śādhi māṁ tvāṁ prapannam || 7 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.007 = MBh_06,024.007). Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: the most beneficial / the highest good

translations.verbatim_quote: Now I am confused about my duty and have lost all composure because of miserly weakness. In this condition I am asking You to tell me for certain what is best for me. Now I am Your disciple, and a soul surrendered unto You. Please instruct me.
I am confused about my duty, and am besieged with anxiety and faintheartedness. I am Your disciple, and am surrendered to You. Please instruct me for certain what is best for me.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: By nature's own way the complete system of material activities is a source of perplexity for everyone. In every step there is perplexity, and therefore it behooves one to approach a bona fide spiritual master who can give one proper guidance for executing the purpose of life.

commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads this verse as the formal moment of upagamana (approach to a teacher) — the point at which Arjuna stops being merely a friend or kinsman to Krishna and becomes a śiṣya (disciple). The verse's structure mirrors the Upaniṣadic injunction: one in doubt about dharma must approach (with prapatti — surrender) a knower, and ask. Without this turn, the teaching cannot be received.

Ramanuja places enormous weight on 'tvāṁ prapannam' — surrendered to You. This phrase, prapatti, is the single most consequential word in this verse for the Śrīvaiṣṇava tradition; it is the formal act of self-surrender that the chapter requires for the teaching to begin. Arjuna does not merely ask for advice; he places himself under the Lord's authority. The chapter's teaching presupposes this surrender.

positions.rendering: the smallness that prevents the candidate from receiving the teaching

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: kārpaṇya is the noun form of kṛpaṇa (a miser) — etymologically connected to kṛpā (compassion). The semantic pivot is interesting: the same root yields both the operator's misplaced 'pity' (kṛpayā in 2.1) and the 'miserly weakness' that now afflicts him. Translators preserve the 'small/petty' force; the commentarial tradition reads it as the smallness that must be admitted before the teaching can land.

positions.rendering: the formal act of self-surrender (prapatti); a technical term in Śrīvaiṣṇava soteriology

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Translation is straightforward; the doctrinal weight differs. For Vishishtadvaita (Ramanuja), prapatti is one of the two paths to liberation and this verse is its scriptural founding moment. For Advaita (Shankara), the surrender is propaedeutic — it readies the disciple to receive the teaching but is not itself the liberating act.

literal_meaning: My nature is overwhelmed by the fault of pity-smallness; my mind is utterly bewildered about dharma. I ask you: tell me with certainty what is best for me. I am your disciple, surrendered to you — instruct me.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The pivotal verse of the chapter and arguably of the entire Gita. Arjuna formally turns from interlocutor to disciple. The verse names three things explicitly: (1) the affective state Arjuna is in (kārpaṇya — pity-smallness), (2) the cognitive state (saṁmūḍha — utterly bewildered about dharma), and (3) the relational shift (śiṣyas te 'ham — I am your disciple). All major commentarial traditions treat this verse as the formal beginning of the teaching: until the student declares surrender and asks, the teaching cannot land. The chapter's voice has been didactic since 2.2; the chapter's pedagogy begins here.
